title: AKOÚŌ v0.4 Expanded Listening Examples
description: Each example is a complete listening output that validates against schemas/listening-output.schema.json.

examples.object_listened_to: Captioned game cutscene with offscreen alarm

heard.statement: The supplied description links an offscreen alarm to a captioned game cutscene.

inferred.statement: The alarm may cue offscreen threat or interface state, but timing requires video/audio inspection.

interpreted.statement: The scene should be read through sound, image, caption, interface, and timing together rather than as audio-only evidence.

undetermined.statement: Frame timing, mix level, caption accuracy, visual content, source, and production intent remain unknown.

what_appears: An offscreen sound can organize a scene it never appears in, through caption, timing, and player expectation.
what_remains_hidden: Actual synchronization, mix decisions, caption authoring, and the player's viewing conditions remain unavailable.

technical: Game engine, mix, and screen-capture chain mediate what the scene makes audible.
cultural: Alarm conventions in games shape how the cue is read.
spatial: Offscreen placement constructs a space the image does not show.
computational: Captions are an authored text layer, not a transparent record of the sound.

hallucination: Do not invent visual content or alarm timing from the description alone.
source_confusion: Synchronization implied by the description must not be treated as real-world causality.

examples.main_reading: The cutscene stages threat through an offscreen alarm whose meaning is co-produced by caption, timing, and interface rather than by the sound alone.
examples.alternative_reading: An accessibility pass could audit whether the caption carries the alarm's urgency for players who do not hear it.

examples.object_listened_to: ASR transcript from a voice-agent demo

heard.statement: The supplied input is an ASR-style transcript from a voice-agent demo.

inferred.statement: The text may represent a speech-to-text stage, but it does not verify vocal quality or speaker identity.

interpreted.statement: The voice-agent workflow should separate vocal sound, transcript, model behavior, consent, and access.

undetermined.statement: Prosody, timbre, accent, speaker identity, consent, ASR accuracy, model stack, and dataset provenance remain unknown.

what_appears: A transcript can be analyzed as text evidence of a speech pipeline without pretending to be the voice itself.
what_remains_hidden: Everything vocal: breath, pacing, overlap, tone, and the body behind the words remain unavailable.

technical: ASR segmentation and formatting decisions shape what the transcript preserves.
cultural: Transcript conventions decide what counts as speech worth writing down.
bodily: The speaking body is absent from the text representation.
computational: Speaker labels are diarization output, not verified identities.

hallucination: Do not reconstruct vocal qualities or emotions from the text.
over_identification: Speaker labels must not become identity claims.
forensic_overreach: Transcript wording must not be treated as verbatim legal evidence.
source_confusion: The transcript is a model representation, not the recording.

examples.main_reading: The transcript is a mediated trace of a speech pipeline; what it shows about the voice-agent stack matters more than what it appears to say about any speaker.
examples.alternative_reading: A transductive pass could map the ASR, turn-taking, and TTS stages the demo implies.

heard.statement: The prompt describes a workflow where alerts are audio-only.

inferred.statement: Users with muted devices, noisy environments, hearing variation, or unavailable speakers may miss alert state changes.
inferred.basis: Design inference from audio-only alert path

interpreted.statement: The workflow implies a default hearing listener and should provide visual, textual, and possibly haptic alternatives.

undetermined.statement: Alert urgency, volume, masking, visual state, haptic support, captioning, user testing, and assistive-technology behavior remain unknown.

what_appears: The implied listener of the design becomes visible: hearing, attentive, in a quiet room, near a speaker.
what_remains_hidden: Real user contexts, assistive-technology behavior, and the consequences of a missed alert remain untested.

technical: Device, speaker, and volume settings stand between the alert and any listener.
cultural: Alert design inherits assumptions about which bodies and environments are normal.
spatial: Room noise and distance condition whether the alert is audible at all.
bodily: Hearing variation, fatigue, and attention load shape access to the alert.

hallucination: Do not assume alert sounds are audible, urgent, or distinct without evidence.
cultural_flattening: Do not speak for disabled users as if their experience were known.
forensic_overreach: Do not claim legal compliance without standards and testing.
aesthetic_overstatement: Do not universalize clarity or urgency across listeners.

examples.main_reading: An audio-only alert path concentrates critical information in a single sensory channel and excludes every context where that channel fails.
examples.alternative_reading: An embodied pass could examine volume, masking, and attention capture if loudness data or user reports become available.

examples.object_listened_to: Long feedback tone in a resonant room

heard.statement: The prompt describes a long feedback tone in a resonant room.

inferred.statement: Sustained feedback and room resonance are possible, but frequency, amplitude, and coupling require measurement.

interpreted.statement: The sound can be approached as an event across loudspeaker, room, air, surfaces, and listener body.

undetermined.statement: Actual waveform, frequency, loudness, room dimensions, speaker position, material composition, and bodily effect remain unknown.
undetermined.basis: No measured signal or room data supplied

what_appears: Duration and resonance foreground the sound as a process unfolding through materials rather than a static object.
what_remains_hidden: The physical system producing the feedback, including gain structure, room modes, and surfaces, remains undescribed.

technical: Microphone-speaker coupling and gain define whether feedback exists at all.
spatial: Room geometry and surfaces shape which frequencies persist.
bodily: Any pressure or vibration claim depends on listener position and playback level.

hallucination: Do not turn described resonance into measured frequencies.
over_identification: Do not name the room, equipment, or materials without evidence.
aesthetic_overstatement: Do not mystify the vibration as universal bodily experience.

examples.main_reading: The description stages a material event in which speaker, air, room, and duration co-produce a tone; its reality as measurement remains entirely open.
examples.alternative_reading: A signal pass on an actual recording could turn the described resonance into measured spectral evidence.
